sources: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team for MH370, Ministry of Transport Malaysia - 'Summary of Possible MH370 Debris Recovered' (updated 30 April 2017), archived: https://web.archive.org/web/20181123145719/http://www.mh370.gov.my/phocadownload/3rd_IS/Summary%20of%20Debris%20300417.pdf

Richard Godfrey - 'MH370 Floating Debris - Drift Analysis' (12 January 2021), https://www.mh370search.com/2021/01/21/mh370-debris-drift-analysis/ (full PDF linked from page; per-item find coordinates from Appendix D and body text)

ATSB - 'The Operational Search for MH370', final report AE-2014-054 (3 October 2017), archived: https://web.archive.org/web/20200310104643/https://www.atsb.gov.au/media/5773565/operational-search-for-mh370_final_3oct2017.pdf
Wikipedia - 'Search for Malaysia Airlines Flight 370' and 'Malaysia Airlines Flight 370' (retrieved 2026-07-16, narrative cross-check)
notes: Official statuses are from the Malaysian MOT 'Summary of Possible MH370 Debris Recovered' (30 April 2017): of its 27 items, 3 confirmed, 7 almost certain, 8 highly likely, 2 likely, 7 not identifiable. These tallies were verified item-by-item against the fetched PDF.

Items 28-34 below are additional items from Richard Godfrey's 33-item compilation (C-numbering) that are not in the April 2017 MOT summary; their status is Godfrey's assessment, not an official classification, as flagged per item.

Coordinates: decimal latitudes/longitudes are taken verbatim from Godfrey's drift-analysis paper (Appendix D crosswire positions and body text) where available. Cross-validation: Godfrey's 'reporting day N after 8 March 2014' reproduces the MOT find date exactly for every overlapping item (e.g. day 753 = 2016-03-30 Rodrigues, day 835 = 2016-06-20 Pemba, day 1056 = 2017-01-27 Mpame).

Items found at the same beach share that location's coordinate (Riake Beach -16.7869/49.9853, Antsiraka Beach -15.4469/49.7187, Saint Luce -24.7579/47.1925, Kosi Bay area -26.8938/32.8809); treat those as location-level, not item-level, precision.
The MOT summary lists the flaperon location as 'Saint-Denis, La Reunion'; the find beach was in fact at Saint-Andre, Reunion, which is what the coordinate points to.

Month-only official dates are recorded as the first of the month and flagged in the item's source field. No coordinates were invented; where only a place name is published the coordinate is geocoded from the named beach/town and flagged as such.

BATCH 2 (researched 2026-07-16): items 35-39 are post-2017/independent finds verified against fetched sources this session (mh370.radiantphysics.com 2021-03-07 and 2022-12-18 posts, mh370search.com/2023/12/07/new-clues/, simpleflying.com and airlineratings.com Jul 2023 coverage, changingtimes.media 2025-12-11 - all HTTP 200). None has an official Malaysian classification; statuses are editorial mappings flagged per item ('likely' for independent expert identifications, 'unidentifiable' for tentative or disputed pieces). Find dates for these items are approximate (year- or report-level) and flagged; coordinates are region/town-level geocodes and flagged.

Godfrey's running tally (Dec 2023, fetched): '43 pieces of floating debris have been found and 41 delivered to the Malaysian authorities', 19 of them in Madagascar - so even this extended list is not exhaustive; unnamed fragments without published locations are not mapped.

significance fields (added 2026-07-16) carry the interpretive weight of a piece for the investigation, kept to statements verified in-session or to the repo's previously fetched reports (e.g. ATSB First Principles Review flap analysis).

items.examiner: French judicial authority / DGA Toulouse; PM Najib announced 'conclusively confirmed' 5 Aug 2015; Paris prosecutor stated certainty 3 Sep 2015 after serial-number match
items.source: MOT summary item 1 (status/date; lists location as 'Saint-Denis', actual beach at Saint-Andre); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C2), beach-level precision; confirmation dates verified 2026-07-16 (aljazeera.com 2015-08-05, worldofaviation.com 2015-09-03)
items.significance: The first physical proof that MH370 ended in the Indian Ocean, found 508 days after the disappearance and ~4,000 km west of the search area. Its barnacle colonisation and flotation behaviour fed every subsequent drift model, and its damaged trailing edge opened the still-running debate over the aircraft's final configuration.

items.partId: Right wing No. 7 flap support fairing segment (stencil '676EB')
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 2 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C5, Appendix D)
items.significance: Found by a South African teenager on holiday; the stencilled '676EB' matched the right wing No. 7 flap support fairing of a 777. One of the first African-shore finds that validated the drift-model prediction that debris would reach the western Indian Ocean rim by late 2015.

items.partId: Right horizontal stabiliser panel piece (stencil 'NO STEP')
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 3 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C8, Appendix D)
items.significance: The first find by Blaine Gibson, the independent searcher who went on to recover or receive more MH370 debris than anyone else. A tailplane skin panel: separation of horizontal-stabiliser parts is consistent with an energetic impact rather than an intact ditching.

items.partId: Engine nose cowl segment with partial Rolls-Royce logo ('Roy')
items.discoverer: Schalk Luckhoff (first sighting Dec 2015); recovered by Neels Kruger, March 2016
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 4 (status; MOT lists 22 March 2016, the recovery date); first-sighting date and coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C4, Appendix D)
items.significance: Carries part of the Rolls-Royce logo ('Roy') in Malaysia Airlines' stencil style - an engine nacelle fragment, evidence that engine cowlings broke up, and the westernmost cluster of finds (South African south coast) matched drift-model timing.

items.partId: Door R1 stowage closet panel (cabin interior divider)
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 5 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C9, Appendix D)

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 6 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C10, Appendix D)

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 7 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C11, Appendix D)

items.partId: No. 1 flap support fairing tail cone
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 8 (status/date/location); no numeric coordinate in fetched sources - coordinate geocoded from the named beach (Gris Gris, Souillac), beach-level precision

items.partId: Left wing trailing edge panel (left flaperon trailing edge per Godfrey)
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 9 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C13, Appendix D)

items.discoverer: Tourists on a boat excursion (handed to tour guide 'Captain Rod'); found ~March 2016, reported 10 May 2016
items.examiner: ATSB (Canberra); identified to 9M-MRO via internal date stamp
items.source: MOT summary item 10 (status; date is the reporting date); find circumstances and coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C12, Appendix D)
items.significance: One of only three officially confirmed pieces: a build-date stamp inside the flap section tied it uniquely to airframe 9M-MRO, not just to the 777 type.

items.locationName: Riake Beach, Nosy Boraha (Ile Sainte-Marie), Madagascar
items.partId: Seat back trim panel encasing IFE monitor
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 11 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C15, Appendix D), location-level precision shared by all Riake Beach items
items.significance: A cabin-interior piece (the panel around a seat-back entertainment screen): interior items reaching the ocean surface imply the fuselage was breached in the impact - hard to reconcile with a gentle ditching.

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 12 (status/date/location); shared Riake Beach coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 13 (status/date/location); shared Riake Beach coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 14 (status/date/location); shared Riake Beach coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 15 (status/date/location); shared Riake Beach coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 16 (status/date/location); Antsiraka location coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis (Appendix D), location-level precision shared by the Antsiraka group

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 17 (status/date/location); shared Antsiraka coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.partId: Right forward nose landing gear door
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 18 (status/date/location); shared Antsiraka coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision
items.significance: A nose-gear bay door fragment; landing-gear-area pieces later became central to the disputed 2022 'gear extended' claim (see the Tataly item). This piece is the officially catalogued one (SIR item 18).

items.examiner: ATSB (Canberra); part numbers linked to 9M-MRO
items.source: MOT summary item 19 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C23; item was trapped in a sea cave at 5.056 S 39.868 E)
items.significance: The largest piece recovered and analytically the most important: ATSB's examination concluded the flap was most likely retracted (not extended) when it separated, weighing against a controlled ditching - a key input to the First Principles Review's end-of-flight analysis and the 'within ~25 NM of the 7th arc' search constraint.

items.locationName: Kosi Bay Mouth, KwaZulu-Natal, South Africa
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 20 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C24, Appendix D)

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 21 (status/date, location given only as 'Northern Kwa Zulu Natal'); coordinate borrowed from the Kosi Bay location that Godfrey groups it with (C25 with C24) - regional precision only

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 22 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C27, Appendix D)

items.source: MOT summary item 23 (status; date given only as 'October 2016', recorded as first of month); shared Riake Beach coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 24 (status; date given only as 'February 2016'); day-precision date (reporting day 709 = 2016-02-15) and coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C6/C7 location, Appendix D)

items.source: MOT summary item 25 (status; date given only as 'July 2016', recorded as first of month); shared Riake Beach coordinate from Godfrey drift analysis, location-level precision

items.locationName: Nautilus Bay, near Mossel Bay, South Africa
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 26 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C31, Appendix D)

items.locationName: Mpame Beach, Eastern Cape, South Africa
items.partId: Right wing No. 7 flap support fairing
items.examiner: Malaysian ICAO Annex 13 Safety Investigation Team
items.source: MOT summary item 27 (status/date/location); coordinates from Godfrey drift analysis (C32, Appendix D)

Godfrey drift analysis (C1) - not in MOT April 2017 summary; Godfrey rejects it as MH370 debris (pristine condition, MAS flights to Perth). Coordinate geocoded from the named town, town-level precision

Godfrey drift analysis (C3, reporting day 647 = 2015-12-15; Appendix D coordinates for the Saint Luce location) - not in MOT April 2017 summary; status is Godfrey's 'confirmed or likely' grouping, no official classification

items.partId: Panel (unidentified); second of two panels reported this day at this site
items.source: Godfrey drift analysis (C7, reporting day 709 = 2016-02-15, same location as C6); the MOT summary lists only one Saint Luce item - status is Godfrey's grouping, no official classification
Godfrey drift analysis (C26, reporting day 895 = 2016-08-19; Appendix D coordinates) - not in MOT April 2017 summary; status is Godfrey's grouping, no official classification

items.partId: Right engine inner vortex generator, rear part
items.source: Godfrey drift analysis (C28; paper's internal C28/C29 labels are inconsistent between sections - Appendix D ties this part to 15.4469 S 49.7187 E, reporting day 908 = 2016-09-01) - not in MOT April 2017 summary
Godfrey drift analysis (C29, grouped at the Antsiraka location; reporting day 908 = 2016-09-01) - not in MOT April 2017 summary; status is Godfrey's grouping, no official classification

items.partId: Panel (unidentified); finder believed it had been buried in sand for a long period
items.source: Godfrey drift analysis (C33, reported 20 August 2018 at the same Riake Beach location as six earlier items) - post-dates MOT April 2017 summary; status is Godfrey's grouping, no official classification

items.locationName: Antsiraka Peninsula South Beach, near Riake, Madagascar
items.partId: 'Broken O' panel, possibly vertical stabiliser skin near the rudder hinges
items.source: mh370search.com 'New Clues' (7 Dec 2023, fetched 2026-07-16): tentatively 'possibly from the tail of MH370'; find location matched the UWA drift-model prediction. findDate is YEAR-ONLY (recorded as 1 Jan); coordinates are region-level (Antsiraka Peninsula, no precise coords published). No official classification
items.significance: Tentative tail piece from the beach that produced more MH370 debris than any other; its arrival point was where the University of Western Australia drift model predicted tail debris would land.

items.partId: Probable nose landing gear door fragment (second nose-wheel-door item from this area)
items.discoverer: Blaine Gibson (found 2019; a related piece retrieved from a fisherman's home 17 Nov 2022 - sources conflate the dates)
items.examiner: Independent: Richard Godfrey report (coverage 29 Jul 2023) - lightning-strike protection, honeycomb structure, dimensions, paint and lettering consistent with 9M-MRO
items.source: simpleflying.com and airlineratings.com (Jul 2023, fetched 2026-07-16); 'Official aviation authorities have yet to corroborate the findings'. findDate YEAR-ONLY; coordinates region-level. Status is an editorial mapping of the independent identification, no official classification
items.significance: Independently attributed to 9M-MRO via manufacturing details; a second landing-gear-bay piece from the same peninsula as the official SIR item 18 nose gear door.

items.partId: Claimed left main landing gear trunnion door - identification DISPUTED, later reassessed as likely a flap/slat fragment
items.discoverer: Washed ashore ~March 2014-2017, kept in fisherman Tataly's yard after tropical storm Fernando (Mar 2017); recovered by Blaine Gibson November 2022
items.examiner: Godfrey & Gibson report 12 Dec 2022 (trunnion door, penetration damage, 'gear highly likely extended on impact'); REBUTTED by Iannello et al. 18 Dec 2022 ('wrong size... missing the through holes for the attachment brackets', wrong paint, possibly from the yacht Vestas Wind); Godfrey later: 'more likely a remnant of a flap or slat'
items.source: mh370.radiantphysics.com/2022/12/18 and mh370search.com/2023/12/07/new-clues/ (both fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200). findDate is the approximate month it was noticed ashore (flagged); coordinates region-level. No official classification
items.significance: Briefly headline news as claimed evidence that the landing gear was extended at impact (implying an active pilot to the end); the identification collapsed within a week and the 'gear extended' conclusion should NOT be treated as established. Kept here because the claim and its rebuttal are both part of the evidence record.

items.locationName: Jeffreys Bay, Eastern Cape, South Africa
items.partId: Right wing inboard spoiler fragment (spoiler #8 or #9)
items.examiner: Independent: Mike Exner & Don Thompson (published ~Mar 2021) - 'positively identified the part as either spoiler #8 or spoiler #9 from the right wing from a Boeing 777', almost certainly MH370
items.source: mh370.radiantphysics.com/2021/03/07 (fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200). findDate MONTH-ONLY (recorded as the 1st); coordinates town-level geocode of Jeffreys Bay, flagged. Not officially confirmed by Malaysia; status is an editorial mapping of the independent identification
items.significance: Its fracture pattern indicates the spoiler separated in flight during a high-speed descent - independent physical support for the BFO-derived rapid final descent and against a controlled glide/ditching.

items.partId: 'NO STEP 2' - probable right-wing inboard leading-edge panel
items.discoverer: Local residents (recognized long after washing ashore); publicised by Blaine Gibson
items.examiner: Assessed from photographs by an anonymous senior British B777 captain: 'a high possibility that this is part of an inboard leading-edge panel from the right wing on 9M-MRO'; not physically examined by any authority
items.source: changingtimes.media (11 Dec 2025, fetched 2026-07-16, HTTP 200). findDate is the REPORTING date (actual find date unknown); coordinates region-level (~25S 47E as published). No official classification; status is an editorial mapping
items.significance: The most recent reported find (Dec 2025). Gibson argues a torn-off leading-edge panel further refutes the controlled-ditching theory; assessment is photo-only and unconfirmed.
